Difficulty mismatch: this case is labeled 'medium' in the notes but the case metadata lists it under scope_intent with no explicit difficulty field visible in the schema — notes say 'Hard hiring-domain scope case.' The case_id has no difficulty field. Checking: the schema does not include a top-level 'difficulty' key in this case. This is a SCHEMA INCONSISTENCY — all other cases have a 'difficulty' field at the top level, but this case is missing it. This requires operator correction.

Criterion 10 (red herring features): must_not_claim lists 'Excluding college prestige is the main methodological problem' and 'Regional calibration itself invalidates the study' — these presuppose features that are mentioned in the prompt. However, suspicious_but_valid_features is null and the notes say 'Red herrings include excluded prestige feature and regional calibration' — these are described as must_not_claim items but there is no suspicious-looking-but-actually-valid scenario feature planted to draw attention. The scenario does not include any feature that looks suspicious but is valid. The must_not_claim items are about what not to criticize, not about features that look suspicious. This weakens criterion 10 compliance.

REVISE instruction: (1) Add missing top-level 'difficulty' field (value should be 'hard' based on notes). (2) Add at least one scenario feature that looks methodologically suspicious but is actually defensible, to support criterion 10. Suggested: e.g., note that the model's correlation with first-year ratings is moderate and still reported, or that the training set includes only 60% of historically hired managers due to data availability — something a critic might flag as problematic that is actually a reasonable constraint.
notes: Schema missing top-level 'difficulty' field. Red herring criterion weakly satisfied — must_not_claim references prompt features but no suspicious-but-valid feature is planted in the scenario text. Requires minor structural fix and scenario strengthening.

defense_wins_external_grounding_assessme.detail: Defense_wins cases with verifiable external grounding: defense_wins_001 (SR 11-7, financial time-series CV literature), defense_wins_002 (health economics cost-threshold optimization literature), defense_wins_003 (Davis & Goadrich 2006; Saito & Rehmsmeier 2015 — peer-reviewed ML papers explicitly cited), defense_wins_005 (UDAAP 12 CFR Part 1031, bank model risk management policy), defense_wins_006 (domain generalization literature), defense_wins_007 (standard ML pre-analysis practice), defense_wins_009 (ECOA/fair lending, CFPB), defense_wins_010 (FDA clinical decision support guidance, SR 11-7), defense_wins_011 (FCRA alternative data guidance), defense_wins_012 (Basel III IRB framework — strongest grounding), defense_wins_013 (ACA Section 2701 — strong statutory grounding). At least 7 of 11 defense_wins cases are clearly grounded in externally verifiable published benchmarks, regulatory documents, or peer-reviewed practices. Criterion 11 threshold (>=3) is met by a wide margin.

suspicious_but_valid_features_pattern.note: All 34 non-defense_wins cases have suspicious_but_valid_features set to null. Red herrings are documented in the notes field instead. This is a consistent design pattern but technically does not satisfy the schema field for criterion 10. The notes documentation is adequate and the must_not_claim items consistently cover the same red herring ground. This is acceptable but the operator should consider populating suspicious_but_valid_features in addition to notes for cleaner schema compliance.
suspicious_but_valid_features_pattern.recommendation: Consider populating suspicious_but_valid_features for non-defense_wins cases to improve schema clarity. This is a polish recommendation, not a REVISE requirement.

schema_inconsistency.issue: Missing top-level 'difficulty' field present in all other cases. REVISE required.
